chunk_summary: Vitamin A prophylaxis and treatment are critical for stunted children to prevent deficiency and support linear growth. Guidelines include prophylactic dosing schedules and therapeutic regimens for clinical deficiency, emphasizing infection control and dietary support.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of vitamin A in preventing stunting and supporting linear growth.
Learn the national prophylactic schedule for vitamin A supplementation in children aged 9 months to 5 years.
Recognize clinical signs of vitamin A deficiency and apply therapeutic dosing regimens.
Counsel caregivers on vitamin A toxicity symptoms and the importance of dietary sources.
key_facts: Prophylactic vitamin A dosing includes 100,000 IU at 9 months and 200,000 IU every 6 months from 16–18 months to 5 years.
Therapeutic dosing for deficiency: 50,000 IU (under 6 months), 100,000 IU (6–11 months), 200,000 IU (12 months and older).
Therapeutic doses are repeated immediately, the next day, and at 2 weeks.
Clinical signs of deficiency include night blindness, conjunctival xerosis, Bitot spots, and corneal changes.
Vitamin A supplementation is often given with measles-containing vaccines at 9 months.
Caregivers should be counseled on transient toxicity symptoms like vomiting and irritability.
reference_quote: Program schedules describe nine prophylactic doses from 9 months to 5 years. A common approach is 100,000 IU at 9 months (often given with a measles-containing vaccine), followed by 200,000 IU every 6 months from 16–18 months until 5 years of age.
document_key_topics: Vitamin A prophylaxis schedule for children
Therapeutic management of vitamin A deficiency
Clinical signs of vitamin A deficiency
Counseling on dietary sources and toxicity symptoms
